title: Singular Asymptotics of SPADE in Quantum Source Discrimination
abstract: We study far-field discrimination between one and two incoherent point sources in the singular regime of weak and closely spaced emitters. Under ideal alignment, spatial-mode demultiplexing (SPADE) attains the quantum-optimal large-sample Stein exponent, but the finite-photon behavior near the one-source boundary and the effect of realistic imperfections remain less understood. Using singular learning theory, we analyze both the aligned and misaligned problems. In the aligned Gaussian case, we derive the zeta-function poles for direct imaging and SPADE, show that both share the same real log canonical threshold $λ=1/2$ but differ in multiplicity, and obtain the corresponding Bayes free-energy asymptotics. This yields a universal subleading advantage of aligned SPADE in the local prior-weighted regime. In the misaligned setting, we study a physically motivated binary-SPADE reduction that retains the full leading $O(s^2)$ leakage contrast near alignment, with corrections from the detailed higher-mode redistribution entering only at $O(s^4)$. We show that misaligned binary-SPADE and direct imaging acquire nontrivial local power on different intrinsic scales, $s=O(n^{-1/4})$ and $s=O(n^{-1/2})$, respectively. However, finite-$n$ Neyman--Pearson comparisons under common physical conditions reveal that direct imaging is stronger on the plotted grids and that misaligned binary-SPADE exhibits an exact blind separation $s^\ast=2θ$, where its power collapses to $α$. These results identify model singularity as a structural organizing principle for finite-photon quantum discrimination and clarify how ideal aligned SPADE benchmarks can fail to translate into finite-$n$ advantages under misalignment.
summary: The paper uses Singular Learning Theory to analyze the asymptotic performance of SPADE versus direct imaging for detecting closely spaced, weak light sources. It reveals that while SPADE is theoretically superior in ideal conditions, detector misalignment can lead to a collapse in detection power and allow direct imaging to perform better in practical finite-photon regimes.
main_problem: Investigating the performance of Spatial-Mode Demultiplexing (SPADE) versus direct imaging for discriminating between one and two incoherent point sources in the singular regime of weak and closely spaced emitters.
main_result: The study identifies that while aligned SPADE has a universal subleading advantage due to different multiplicity in the singular expansion, misaligned SPADE can be outperformed by direct imaging in finite-photon regimes and exhibits a 'blind separation' point where power collapses.
method: Application of Singular Learning Theory (SLT) to analyze zeta-function poles, real log canonical thresholds, and Bayes free-energy asymptotics, supplemented by Monte Carlo simulations.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional diffraction-limited imaging system with a Gaussian point-spread function (PSF) used for discriminating between a single source (null) and two incoherent sources (alternative) with relative brightness epsilon and separation s.
key_observables: Log Bayes factor, Bayes free-energy difference, mode labels (q) in SPADE, and detection positions (Xi) in direct imaging.
important_parameters: Relative brightness (epsilon), spatial separation (s), detector misalignment (theta), number of photons (n), and PSF width (sigma).
main_assumptions: Assumes a Gaussian PSF and uses a physically motivated reduction of full SPADE to a binary-SPADE model in the sub-Rayleigh regime.
figures_summary: The figures likely show comparisons of Bayes free energy and power between direct imaging and SPADE, including plots of the 'blind separation' effect and the scaling of power on different intrinsic scales.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the source discrimination problem, defines the aligned and misaligned models, applies Singular Learning Theory to derive asymptotic expansions for both cases, validates these asymptotics with numerical simulations, and concludes with the physical implications of model singularity.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum sensing as it provides a rigorous mathematical framework (SLT) to understand why ideal quantum-optimal benchmarks (like SPADE) may fail to provide advantages in practical, misaligned, finite-photon scenarios.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Finite-n power under common physical conditions for direct imaging and the misaligned binary-SPADE model. Panels (a)–(c): power versus physical separation s for n = 200, 500, 2000; panel (d): power versus sample size n for fixed separations s = 0.05, 0.10, 0.20. Parameters are σ = 1, ϵ = 0.3, θ = 0.1, and α = 0.05. For binary-SPADE, the finite-n power is computed exactly from the binomial model using the randomized Neyman–Pearson test. For direct imaging, the power is evaluated by common-random-number Monte Carlo.
